narrative: Dear [Student's Name], your law school application was unfortunately rejected because our predictive model estimated a 57% chance that you would fail the bar exam on your first try, based on patterns from thousands of past students. This prediction draws from key aspects of your profile, including your third-year school decile ranking of 2 (well below the typical 5.5), your first-year decile of 3 (versus an average of about 5.7), a notably low LSAT score of 24 (compared to the usual 37), an undergraduate GPA of 2.6 (lower than the average 3.2), middle family income, being male, White, and planning full-time enrollment. These details stood out because they align more closely with students who struggled on the bar than those who passed. The strongest factor pushing the model toward predicting failure was your low third-year decile ranking, as it suggested academic challenges that often carry over to bar performance. Right behind that was your first-year decile, which reinforced concerns about early inconsistencies in your school grades. Your LSAT score had the next biggest influence, standing out as much lower than what successful bar takers typically show. Following that, your middle income bracket, undergraduate GPA, racial background as White, full-time plans, and gender as male each nudged the prediction slightly further toward failure, though with less weight—mostly because your demographics are quite common in the dataset. Overall, your academic metrics were the heavy hitters here, placing you below average in ways that the model links strongly to bar exam risks. Compared to typical admitted students, who boast higher deciles, LSATs around 37, and GPAs over 3.2, your profile signaled higher odds of needing multiple bar attempts. To boost your future chances, focus on retaking the LSAT to aim for a score closer to 35 or above, as that's one area you can directly improve with dedicated prep. Strengthening your academic habits now—perhaps through targeted tutoring to lift any ongoing grade deciles—could also help demonstrate upward trends. While family income and demographics are harder to shift, excelling in these changeable academic factors has turned predictions around for many similar students. We encourage you to reapply after addressing these, as even modest gains can tip the scales toward a passing forecast. Keep pushing—you've got the determination to rewrite this story.
